original_abstract:
This paper presents efficient numerical implementations for the $\delta$-Generalized Labeled Multi-Bernoulli ($\delta$-GLMB) filter, an analytic solution to the multi-target Bayes recursion that provides joint estimation of target states and trajectories. While the $\delta$-GLMB filter offers a rigorous closed-form solution, its direct implementation is challenged by a number of hypotheses that grows super-exponentially over time. To address this, we propose a strategy to truncate the multi-target densities by selecting only the most significant hypotheses without exhaustive computation. 

Specifically, we utilize ranked assignment algorithms to manage the update step and the $K$-shortest path algorithm to handle the prediction step. To further enhance computational efficiency, we introduce look-ahead strategies based on the Probability Hypothesis Density (PHD) filter to prioritize the most relevant components. Furthermore, we provide a theoretical characterization of the truncation process, proving that selecting components with the highest weights minimizes the $L_1$-error in the multi-target density. Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of these algorithms in tracking a time-varying number of targets in the presence of clutter and detection uncertainty, showing superior performance and robustness against the "spooky effect" compared to existing methods like the Cardinalized PHD (CPHD) filter.
humanized_abstract:
Abstract— In this paper, we investigate efficient numerical implementations for the δ-Generalized Labeled Multi-Bernoulli (δ-GLMB) filter, which is an analytical solution to the multi-target Bayes recursion under the generalized LMB assumption. The motivation behind the development of the δ-GLMB filter is the joint tracking of state and trajectory of various targets, and as such, the corresponding multi-target implementation poses a number of hypotheses that grow exponentially and, even more so, super-exponentially in time, which makes the direct implementation of the resultant δ-GLMB filter highly inefficient and in some cases may not be feasible. In this paper, we show that it is possible to carry out a meaningful pruning of the multi-target density without fully searching through all possible combinations of the target tracks.

We apply ranked assignment algorithms to the update step and the $K$-shortest path algorithm to the prediction step. To further reduce the computation time, we also apply look-ahead strategies that utilize information from the PHD filter to select the more important components. We derive a characterization of the truncation process and show that selecting the highest weighted components leads to the smallest $L_1$-error in the multi-target density. Finally, simulations are provided that confirm the merits of the algorithms in handling a varying number of targets, while dealing with clutter and detection uncertainties. The proposed algorithms clearly outperform the CPHD filter in reducing the notorious "spooky effect".

document.output:

In this paper, we show that it is possible to carry out a meaningful pruning of the multi-target density without fully searching through all possible combinations of the target tracks.

We apply ranked assignment algorithms to the update step and the $K$-shortest path algorithm to the prediction step. Finally, simulations are provided that confirm the merits of the algorithms in handling a varying number of targets, while dealing with clutter and detection uncertainties. The proposed algorithms clearly outperform the CPHD filter in reducing the notorious "spooky effect".
document.input:
This paper presents efficient numerical implementations for the $\delta$-Generalized Labeled Multi-Bernoulli ($\delta$-GLMB) filter, an analytic solution to the multi-target Bayes recursion that provides joint estimation of target states and trajectories.
